body_text:
esidue Theory
36. If $g\left(z_{0}\right) \neq 0$ and $h$ has a zero of order 2 at $z_{0}$, then
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{g(z)}{h(z)}, z_{0}\right)=2 \frac{g^{\prime}\left(z_{0}\right)}{h^{\prime \prime}\left(z_{0}\right)}-\frac{2}{3} \frac{g\left(z_{0}\right) h^{\prime \prime \prime}\left(z_{0}\right)}{\left[h^{\prime \prime}\left(z_{0}\right)\right]^{2}}
$$
37. Project Problem: Laplace transform of Bessel functions. In this you will use residues to derive the formula
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} J_{n}(t) e^{-s t} d t=\frac{1}{\sqrt{s^{2}+1}}\left(\sqrt{s^{2}+1}-s\right)^{n}, \quad s>0
$$
where $J_{n}(t)$ is the Bessel function of integer order $n \geq 0$. This formula g Laplace transform of $J_{n}(t)$. To illustrate the useful method that is involve project and for clarity's sake, we start with the case $n=0$.
(a) Using the integral representation of $J_{0}$ from Exercise 35(a), Section 4.
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} J_{0}(t) e^{-s t} d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi i} \int_{0}^{\infty} \int_{C_{1}(0)} e^{-t\left(s-\frac{1}{2}\left(\zeta-\frac{1}{\zeta}\right)\right)} \frac{d \zeta}{\zeta} d t
$$
(You should not be bothered by a double integral involving a contour integ, contour integral can be parametrized and transformed into a definite integ the interval $[0,2 \pi]$, as shown in Exercise 36, Section 4.5. But this will advantageous here.)
(b) Show that for $\zeta$ on $C_{1}(0)$, the complex number $\zeta-\frac{1}{\zeta}$ is purely imagin conclude that for real $s$ and $t$
$$
\left|e^{-t\left(s-\frac{1}{2}\left(\zeta-\frac{1}{\zeta}\right)\right)}\right|=e^{-t s}
$$
(c) Thus the integral in (a) is absolutely convergent. Although we will not details here, this fact is enough to justify interchanging the order of inte Interchange the order of integration, evaluate the integral in $t$ and obtain
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} J_{0}(t) e^{-s t} d t=\frac{1}{\pi i} \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{d \zeta}{-\zeta^{2}+2 s \zeta+1}, \quad s>0
$$
(d) Evaluate the integral using the residue theorem and derive
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} J_{0}(t) e^{-s t} d t=\frac{1}{\sqrt{s^{2}+1}}, \quad s>0
$$
(e) Proceed as we did in (a)-(d), using the integral representation for $J_{r}$ show that for $s>0$
$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} J_{n}(t) e^{-s t} d t=\frac{1}{\pi i} \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{d \zeta}{\left(-\zeta^{2}+2 s \zeta+1\right) \zeta^{n}}=\frac{1}{\pi i} \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{\eta^{n} d \eta}{\eta^{2}+2 s \eta}
$$
[Hint: To justify the second equality, let $\zeta=\frac{1}{\eta}, d \zeta=-\frac{d \eta}{\eta^{2}}$. As $\zeta$ runs $C_{1}(0), \eta$ runs through the reverse of $C_{1}(0)$.]
(f) Derive (11) by computing the integral in (e) using residues.
